topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with a grating monotony. Sarah stared at the blueprint spread across the steel table, her jaw tight. It was the third time this week she’d found the identical schematic for the 'Chrono-Lattice' prototype, complete with the same maddeningly convoluted energy matrix. She picked up a wrench, turning it over and over in her hand until the metal felt slick. "Are you serious?" she finally managed, the sound brittle in the quiet space. Across the room, Mark was meticulously aligning the same set of micro-regulators. He didn't look up, his movements precise and unnervingly calm. Sarah let out a slow, defeated breath, pushing the blueprint away as if it were physically repulsive. This was becoming a deliberate, infuriating joke played on her sanity.

[story 2]

"This is absurd," muttered David, rubbing his temples hard enough to leave red marks on his skin. He leaned back from the workbench, the cheap plastic chair protesting beneath his weight. The data logs confirmed it: the core algorithms, the structural integrity calculations—every single line matched the files he’d spent the last six months developing. He slammed his palm flat onto the desk, the vibration rattling the tools. He watched Clara across the room, her brow furrowed in concentration over a screen displaying the exact same iterative design. He felt a familiar tightening in his chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure building behind his eyes. He sighed, a long, drawn-out sound of profound weariness. "We're running in circles," he said, his voice flat, devoid of any real urgency.

[story 3]

I dropped the soldering iron onto the bench with a clatter that echoed too loudly in the cavernous workshop. I felt a sudden, hot flush creep up my neck. It was the fourth iteration of the 'Aetheric Conduit' design, and it was exactly the same as the one I’d been wrestling with for weeks. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the strands stick to my scalp with dampness. I glared at Ben, who was currently tightening a bolt on a completely identical housing unit, his focus absolute. "Seriously?" I asked, the disbelief thick in my tone. I didn't move toward him; instead, I walked to the window, staring out at the gray cityscape, wishing the glass would just shatter. The sheer repetition of it all was starting to feel physically draining.
